principles.name: Body-First Framing
principles.how: Open every video by stating the extreme physical or emotional experience you will put yourself through, not the object you will build or test.
principles.why: Viewers swipe away from object-focused process videos; they stay for human stakes, bodily risk, and personal transformation.

principles.name: No Deferred Payoffs
principles.how: Deliver the title's central promise with a visible, personal result before the final 10 seconds; never end with 'I'll update you later' or 'Part 2 coming soon' as the only resolution.
principles.why: Worst videos defer the climax to future uploads, training viewers to swipe; best videos complete a full emotional arc inside the current video.

principles.name: Present-Tense Survival Mode
principles.how: Narrate every beat as if it is happening right now ('my legs are tightening,' 'I am terrified') rather than reflecting on what happened yesterday.
principles.why: Present tense creates immediacy and parasocial presence; past tense feels like a detached recap that invites skipping.

principles.name: Sensory Substitution
principles.how: Replace every technical specification (PSI, material name, CAD software) with the physical sensation it causes on your body (pressure, numbness, fear of breaking).
principles.why: Technical specs are abstract and skipable; sensory details trigger mirror neurons and force viewers to feel the experience.

principles.name: Micro-Conflict Cadence
principles.how: Insert a new obstacle, surprise, or escalation every 2-3 sentences; use 'but,' 'however,' or 'then this happened' as mandatory transition words between beats.
principles.why: Continuous micro-conflicts prevent the lulls where swipes happen; worst videos have long process explanations without stakes.

principles.name: Vulnerability-First Hook
principles.how: Within the first 15 seconds, confess a fear, shame, weakness, or mistake ('I was terrified,' 'I got called fat,' 'I failed last time').
principles.why: Competence is boring; vulnerability creates instant relatability and curiosity about how you will overcome it.

principles.name: Experiential Framing
principles.how: Replace 'I wanted to make/build' with 'I wanted to see what would happen to me if...' in every hook and transition.
principles.why: Maker framing promises a process; experiential framing promises a transformation. Viewers retain transformation, not instruction.

principles.name: Quantified Personal Toll
principles.how: Use exact numbers to describe your body or effort (e.g., '33,880 steps,' '144 lbs,' '217 push-ups,' '7,000 calories') instead of vague intensifiers.
principles.why: Specific numbers make the pain feel real and measurable; 'a lot' or 'very hard' is forgettable and swipeable.

principles.name: Segment Cliffhangers
principles.how: End every 25% segment with an explicit reason the viewer must keep watching ('but there's more,' 'this is where things took a turn,' 'you won't believe what happened next').
principles.why: Best videos maintain open loops at every quarter mark; worst videos let scenes resolve without forward momentum.

principles.name: Narrative Integration of Sponsors
principles.how: Introduce the sponsor as a tool that enables the extreme challenge or solves a survival problem within the story, not as a separate endorsement after the action.
principles.why: Detached CTAs signal the video is over; integrated tools feel like plot points that extend the adventure and maintain flow.

principles.name: Expectation Violation Hooks
principles.how: Start with a common assumption ('you'd probably think this is easy,' 'these shoes are supposed to make you faster') then immediately violate it with a contradictory result.
principles.why: Pattern interruption in the first 3 seconds hacks the brain's predictive coding and stops the swipe reflex.

principles.name: Failure Before Success
principles.how: Script at least one visible failure, humiliation, or physical breakdown before any achievement or positive result.
principles.why: Success without struggle is unsatisfying; struggle creates suspense and makes the payoff feel earned rather than given.

principles.name: Second-Person Direct Address
principles.how: Use 'you'd probably think,' 'if you've ever wondered,' or 'you can see' at least once in the hook to pull the viewer into your perspective.
principles.why: Direct address creates a conversational bond; detached narration feels like a lecture and invites swiping.

principles.name: Breathless Fragment Pacing
principles.how: Write sentences under 10 words; use verbal fragments ('Two pounds. Just like that.') and avoid complex subordinate clauses.
principles.why: Short fragments force a fast read that matches the dopamine pace of short-form; long sentences feel like work.

principles.name: Explicit Emotion Labeling
principles.how: Name the exact emotion you feel in the moment ('I was embarrassed,' 'this felt relaxing,' 'I got frustrated') rather than describing the scene neutrally.
principles.why: Emotional labeling helps viewers mirror your state; neutral descriptions require them to interpret, which costs attention.

principles.name: Transformation Titles
principles.how: Title the video as a personal transformation or extreme bodily test ('I became...,' 'What X did to my body,' 'I did...') rather than an object description ('Making...,' 'Testing...').
principles.why: Object titles attract makers; transformation titles attract everyone. The algorithm rewards broad retention over niche interest.

principles.name: Escalation Transitions
principles.how: Use 'but then,' 'however,' 'this is where it got crazy,' or 'the real surprise came' to bridge every beat; never use 'next' or 'then I did.'
principles.why: 'Next' implies predictable sequence; 'but then' implies chaos and curiosity, which holds viewers through uncertainty.

principles.name: Zero Jargon
principles.how: Ban words like 'CAD,' '3D printed,' 'carbon fiber,' 'SolidWorks,' or 'PSI' from the script; translate every mechanic into human impact.
principles.why: Jargon excludes casual viewers and signals a slow process video; human impact includes everyone and feels immediate.

principles.name: Earned Sequel Tease
principles.how: If teasing Part 2, first deliver a full satisfying result (a winner, a number, a visible change) in the current video, then add the tease as a bonus.
principles.why: Worst videos use 'to be continued' as a substitute for an ending; best videos use it as an upsell after delivering value.

principles.name: Hard Deadline Anchor
principles.how: Establish a non-negotiable time limit in the first 5 seconds ('before the plane lands,' 'in 24 hours,' 'in one minute').
principles.why: Time pressure creates automatic suspense; open-ended projects feel optional and easily swipeable.

principles.name: Real-Time Body Changes
principles.how: Show and narrate your body changing during the video (weight on a scale, blisters forming, heart racing, legs giving out) rather than summarizing at the end.
principles.why: Real-time transformation is visual proof that keeps viewers watching for the next physical change.

principles.name: Forced Action Verbs
principles.how: Replace 'I decided to' with 'I was forced to,' 'I had to,' or 'I couldn't stop' to increase narrative urgency.
principles.why: Decisions feel optional; forced actions feel inevitable. Inevitability holds attention because escape is uncertain.

principles.name: Earned CTA
principles.how: Make the call-to-action a result of the challenge ('I earned 1,700 Sweatcoins, you can get the free app') rather than a begging ask ('Subscribe to help me reach 4M').
principles.why: Earned CTAs feel like value sharing; begging triggers the swipe reflex because it signals the story is over.

principles.name: Mystery Pronoun Payoff
principles.how: Use 'THIS' or 'what happened' as a noun to create curiosity gaps ('and THIS happened,' 'watch what this did to my body').
principles.why: Vague demonstrative pronouns force the brain to fill the gap by watching; specificity in the hook kills curiosity.

principles.name: Ensemble Reactions
principles.how: Include at least one other person speaking or reacting in the first 10 seconds to create social stakes and break up solo narration.
principles.why: Solo process narration is monotonous; dialogue and reactions add texture, authenticity, and social proof of the stakes.

principles.name: Three-Sentence Process Cap
principles.how: Never explain how something is built or works for more than 3 consecutive sentences; immediately return to the bodily test or consequence.
principles.why: Process explanations are where swipes happen; best videos treat mechanics as speed bumps, not destinations.

principles.name: Transformation Ending
principles.how: End on a physical or emotional transformation reveal (show the dirty socks, the final weight, the exhausted body) or a shocking consequence, not a verbal promise.
principles.why: Visual transformation is proof of payoff; verbal promises feel unfinished and train viewers to swipe next time.

principles.name: Myth-Busting Frame
principles.how: Frame the video as testing a specific myth or assumption ('apparently these make you jump higher,' 'you'd think meditation is easy').
principles.why: Myths give the viewer a hypothesis to test alongside you, creating active engagement rather than passive observation.

principles.name: Micro Three-Act Structure
principles.how: Structure even 60-second videos with three acts: Setup (the claim), Crisis (the unexpected pain/problem), Resolution (the twist or result).
principles.why: Stories with shape feel complete; shapeless process videos feel like homework that can be abandoned at any point.

principles.name: Concrete Antagonist
principles.how: Name the active enemy (asthma, the cold, a rival, equipment failure, blisters) and personify it as a force working against you.
principles.why: Conflict requires an antagonist; videos without one feel like tutorials rather than stories with stakes.

principles.name: No Subscription Begging
principles.how: Eliminate 'subscribe,' 'follow for updates,' or 'help me reach X million' from the final 5 seconds; replace with story resolution.
principles.why: Subscription begging signals the content is over and trains viewers to leave; story resolution trains them to rewatch.

principles.name: Personal Hyperbole
principles.how: Use extreme but personal comparisons ('like a superhero,' 'the most painful thing I've ever done,' 'impossible') tied to your own experience, not generic hype.
principles.why: Generic hype is ignored; personal hyperbole is authentic and sets a high stakes bar that demands verification.

principles.name: Five-Second Brand Rule
principles.how: Keep any brand mention under 5 seconds and immediately tie it to an observable outcome ('the noise canceling blocked the wind').
principles.why: Long brand reads feel like ads and trigger swipe; brief, outcome-tied mentions feel like plot devices.

principles.name: In Media Res Opening
principles.how: Start with the most extreme moment of the experience (the jump, the shock, the fall) and only rewind 3-5 seconds for context if needed.
principles.why: Starting at the peak creates instant curiosity about how you got there; slow builds lose impatient scrollers.

principles.name: Rule of Three Attempts
principles.how: Script escalating attempts in threes: first attempt fails or is underwhelming, second is harder/worse, third produces the unexpected twist.
principles.why: The Rule of 3 creates a satisfying rhythm and builds anticipation for the final variation.

principles.name: Conversational Roughness
principles.how: Preserve verbal roughness ('um,' 'like,' breathlessness, interrupted thoughts) rather than polishing into smooth narration.
principles.why: Polished narration feels pre-recorded and detached; roughness feels like a real friend FaceTiming you.

principles.name: Co-Conspirator Address
principles.how: Share secret realizations with the viewer ('I realized something,' 'I had an idea,' 'I noticed a problem nobody saw') to make them feel inside your head.
principles.why: Insider status creates loyalty; external observation creates distance that makes swiping easier.

principles.name: No Retrospective Language
principles.how: Ban phrases like 'looking back,' 'in hindsight,' or 'eventually I learned'; stay in the uncertainty of the present moment.
principles.why: Retrospection implies you survived and know the answer, killing suspense; present uncertainty keeps viewers hooked.

principles.name: Sensory Adjective Priority
principles.how: Use cold, slippery, dizzy, freezing, smoky, numb, and loud instead of strong, big, advanced, or high-quality.
principles.why: Sensory words activate the body; evaluative words are abstract and skipable.

principles.name: Build-to-Test Ratio
principles.how: If showing a build, limit it to 10% of the runtime and only use it to set up the bodily test that follows.
principles.why: Build footage is process; test footage is story. Best videos invert the maker-channel ratio to prioritize story.

principles.name: Self-Testing Reframe
principles.how: Replace 'I built X' with 'I put myself through X' or 'I tested X on my own body' in every title and hook.
principles.why: Self-testing implies risk and transformation; building implies craft and time, which swipers avoid.

principles.name: Visual Payoff Close
principles.how: Make the final 10 seconds a visual reveal (the dirty socks, the broken shoes, the final push-up count) rather than a verbal summary.
principles.why: The brain processes visual proof faster than text; verbal summaries feel like credits rolling.

principles.name: Dialogue Snippets
principles.how: Drop in off-camera voices, friend reactions, or brief exchanges ('Mom, I don't think that's the best plan') to break narration.
principles.why: Pure narration fatigues; dialogue resets attention and adds social proof of the stakes.

principles.name: Emotional Scoreboard
principles.how: When comparing two things (with/without, USA vs Canada), keep the scoreboard personal ('my legs hurt more,' 'I felt slower') rather than purely metric.
principles.why: Metrics alone are sterile; personal emotion makes the scoreboard matter and feel relatable.

principles.name: Open Mistake Policy
principles.how: Admit at least one miscalculation, embarrassment, or failure openly; never present yourself as fully prepared or expert.
principles.why: Competence kills curiosity; mistakes create suspense and relatability that hold viewers through the arc.

principles.name: Narrative CTA Escalation
principles.how: If selling a product, frame it as the natural next level of the challenge ('to take it to the next level, we built a website/company').
principles.why: CTAs that feel like plot continuation maintain momentum; CTAs that feel like commercials end the story.

principles.name: Surprise Discovery Structure
principles.how: Replace step-by-step instructions with surprise discoveries narrated in real time ('and then I noticed something I didn't expect').
principles.why: Steps are predictable; discoveries are not. Unpredictability is the currency of retention.

principles.name: Inclusive We
principles.how: Use 'we' when including friends or team to share failure and amplify camaraderie ('we calculated,' 'we brought in water balloons').
principles.why: 'We' diffuses individual failure and creates ensemble energy; 'I' alone can feel isolating or arrogant.

principles.name: Failure-Frame Incomplete
principles.how: If the result is genuinely incomplete, frame it as a catastrophic or funny failure rather than a neutral 'to be continued.'
principles.why: A funny failure is still a payoff; a neutral tease is a broken promise that trains viewers to swipe next time.

principles.name: Title-Payoff Contract
principles.how: Ensure the final frame physically shows the exact promise made in the title/thumbnail (the white socks are dirty, the date is won/lost, the steps are counted).
principles.why: Viewers swipe when they feel cheated by the title; delivering the exact visual proof builds trust and rewatch.
